The [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Federal Republic of Germany") is a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation") and consists of sixteen partly sovereign "[states](/wiki/Federated_state "Federated state")" ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Länder*, [sing.](/wiki/Grammatical_number#Overview "Grammatical number") *Land*).[[a]](#cite_note-1)[[1]](#cite_note-2)[[2]](#cite_note-3) Of the 16 states, 13 are so-called "area-states" (*Flächenländer*); in these, below the level of the state government, there is a division into local authorities (counties and county-level cities) that have their own administration. Two states, [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") and [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), are [city-states](/wiki/Independent_city#Stadtstaaten "Independent city"), in which there is no separation between state government and local administration. The state of [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)") is a special case: the state consists of the cities of [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(city) "Bremen (city)"), for which the state government also serves as the municipal administration, and [Bremerhaven](/wiki/Bremerhaven "Bremerhaven"), which has its own local administration separate from the state government. It is therefore a mixture of a city-state and an area-state.
Three states, [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony"), and [Thuringia](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia"), use the appellation *[Freistaat](/wiki/Freistaat "Freistaat")* ("free state"); this title is merely stylistic and carries no legal or political significance (similar to the [US states that call themselves a commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_(U.S._state) "Commonwealth (U.S. state)")).

The [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") ("West Germany") was created in 1949 through the unification of the three western zones previously under American, British, and French administration in the [aftermath of World War II](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_II "Aftermath of World War II"). Initially, the states of the Federal Republic were [Baden](/wiki/South_Baden "South Baden") (until 1952), [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") (*Bayern*), [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)"), [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") (*Hessen*), [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony") (*Niedersachsen*), [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia") (*Nordrhein-Westfalen*), [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate") (*Rheinland-Pfalz*), [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein"), [Württemberg-Baden](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Baden "Württemberg-Baden") (until 1952), and [Württemberg-Hohenzollern](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Hohenzollern "Württemberg-Hohenzollern") (until 1952). [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin"), while still under occupation by the Western Allies, viewed itself as part of the Federal Republic and was largely integrated and considered a *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* state. In 1952, following [a referendum](/wiki/1951_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg_referendum "1951 Baden-Württemberg referendum"), Baden, Württemberg-Baden, and Württemberg-Hohenzollern merged into [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg"). In 1957, the [Saar Protectorate](/wiki/Saar_Protectorate "Saar Protectorate") joined the Federal Republic as the state of [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland").

The next major change occurred with [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification") in 1990, in which the territory of the former German Democratic Republic ([East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany")) became part of the Federal Republic, by accession of the [re-established eastern states](/wiki/New_states_of_Germany "New states of Germany") of [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg"), Mecklenburg-West Pomerania (*[Mecklenburg-Vorpommern](/wiki/Mecklenburg-Vorpommern "Mecklenburg-Vorpommern")*), Saxony (*Sachsen*), [Saxony-Anhalt](/wiki/Saxony-Anhalt "Saxony-Anhalt") (*Sachsen-Anhalt*), and Thuringia (*Thüringen*), and the reunification of West and [East Berlin](/wiki/East_Berlin "East Berlin") into a city state. A referendum in 1996 to merge Berlin with surrounding Brandenburg failed to reach the necessary majority vote in Brandenburg, while a majority of Berliners voted in favour.

## States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: States")]

It was the individual states that formed the Federal Republic of Germany in 1949. This contrasted with post-war developments in [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), where the national federation (*Bund*) was established first, and the individual states were subsequently defined as units within that federal framework.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The German use of the term *Länder* ("lands") dates back to the [Weimar Constitution](/wiki/Weimar_Constitution "Weimar Constitution") of 1919. Previously, the [states of the German Empire](/wiki/States_of_the_German_Empire "States of the German Empire") had been called *Staaten* ("states"). Today, it is common to use the term *Bundesland* (federated *Land*).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Officially, the term "*Bundesland*" appears in neither the 1919 constitution nor the current one. Three *Länder* call themselves *[Freistaaten](/wiki/Freistaat "Freistaat")* ("free states", an older German term for "republic"): Bavaria (since 1919), Saxony (originally from 1919 and again since 1990), and Thuringia (since 1994). Of the 17 states at the end of the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic"), six still exist (though partly with different borders):

* [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") (The geographically distinct [Palatinate](/wiki/Circle_of_the_Rhine "Circle of the Rhine"), which was part of Bavaria, 1816–1946, is now part of [Rheinland Pfalz](/wiki/Rheinland_Pfalz "Rheinland Pfalz")).
* [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)")
* [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg")
* [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse")
* [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony")
* [Thuringia](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia")

The other 11 states of the Weimar Republic either merged into one another or were separated into smaller entities:

* [Anhalt](/wiki/Free_State_of_Anhalt "Free State of Anhalt") is now part of the state of [Saxony-Anhalt](/wiki/Saxony-Anhalt "Saxony-Anhalt").
* [Baden](/wiki/Republic_of_Baden "Republic of Baden") is now part of [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg").
* [Braunschweig](/wiki/Free_State_of_Brunswick "Free State of Brunswick") is now part of [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony").
* [Lippe](/wiki/Free_State_of_Lippe "Free State of Lippe") is now part of [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia").
* [Lübeck](/wiki/Free_City_of_L%C3%BCbeck "Free City of Lübeck") is now part of [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein").
* [Mecklenburg-Schwerin](/wiki/Free_State_of_Mecklenburg-Schwerin "Free State of Mecklenburg-Schwerin") and [Mecklenburg-Strelitz](/wiki/Free_State_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Free State of Mecklenburg-Strelitz") are now parts of [Mecklenburg-Vorpommern](/wiki/Mecklenburg-Vorpommern "Mecklenburg-Vorpommern").
* [Oldenburg](/wiki/Free_State_of_Oldenburg "Free State of Oldenburg") is now part of [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony"), with its former exclaves now belonging to their neighbouring states of [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate") and [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein").
* [Prussia](/wiki/Free_State_of_Prussia "Free State of Prussia") was [divided](/wiki/Abolition_of_Prussia "Abolition of Prussia") among the states of [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg"), [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony"), [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia"), [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate"), [Saxony-Anhalt](/wiki/Saxony-Anhalt "Saxony-Anhalt") and [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein"). The erstwhile [Prussian provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Prussia "Provinces of Prussia") of [Brandenburg](/wiki/Province_of_Brandenburg "Province of Brandenburg"), [Saxony](/wiki/Province_of_Saxony "Province of Saxony"), Schleswig-Holstein and [Hanover](/wiki/Province_of_Hanover "Province of Hanover") formed the core of the states of Brandenburg, Saxony-Anhalt, Schleswig-Holstein and Lower Saxony, respectively. The Prussian provinces of [Westphalia](/wiki/Province_of_Westphalia "Province of Westphalia") and [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhine_Province "Rhine Province") contributed most territory to the state of North Rhine-Westphalia, and Rhineland province contributed about half of the territory of the state of Rhineland Palatinate. Most of the Prussian province of [Hesse-Nassau](/wiki/Province_of_Hesse-Nassau "Province of Hesse-Nassau") was merged with the existing state of Hesse.   
  Some territories bordering other states were annexed to the bordering state. Also, Prussia had exclaves that were surrounded by other states. These became part of their surrounding states. All states, except [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), now have territory of the former Free State of Prussia. Other former Prussian territories lying east of the rivers [Neisse](/wiki/Neisse "Neisse") and [Oder](/wiki/Oder "Oder") were lost in 1945 and are now part of [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") or [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"). They are Silesia (Upper and Lower), Pomerania, West Prussia-Posen, and East Prussia respectively.
* [Schaumburg-Lippe](/wiki/Free_State_of_Schaumburg-Lippe "Free State of Schaumburg-Lippe") is now part of [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony").
* [Württemberg](/wiki/Free_People%27s_State_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Free People's State of Württemberg") is now part of [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg").

Possible boundary changes between states continue to be debated in Germany, in contrast to how there are "significant differences among the American states and regional governments in other federations without serious calls for territorial changes" in those other countries.[[3]](#cite_note-4) Arthur B. Gunlicks summarizes the main arguments for boundary reform in Germany: "the German system of dual federalism requires strong *Länder* that have the administrative and fiscal capacity to implement legislation and pay for it from own source revenues. Too many *Länder* also make coordination among them and with the federation more complicated."[[4]](#cite_note-5) But several proposals have failed so far; territorial reform remains a controversial topic in German politics and public perception.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [List of historic states of Germany](/wiki/List_of_historic_states_of_Germany "List of historic states of Germany"), [States of the German Empire](/wiki/States_of_the_German_Empire "States of the German Empire"), and [States of the Weimar Republic](/wiki/States_of_the_Weimar_Republic "States of the Weimar Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/German_Empire_states_map.svg/250px-German_Empire_states_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:German_Empire_states_map.svg)

The [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") (light gray) within the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") (1871–1918)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Weimar_Republic_states_map.svg/250px-Weimar_Republic_states_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Weimar_Republic_states_map.svg)

The [states of the Weimar Republic](/wiki/States_of_the_Weimar_Republic "States of the Weimar Republic") in 1925, with the [Free State of Prussia](/wiki/Free_State_of_Prussia "Free State of Prussia") as the largest

[Federalism](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism") has a long tradition in German history. Until the early 19th century, the majority of the territory that later became Germany was part of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), which in 1796 was made up of more than 300 individual political entities subject to the [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") in Vienna. The number of states was greatly reduced during the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") (1796–1814), and the Empire itself was abolished in 1806. The [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna"), which restructured Europe after the wars, created the highly federalized 39-state [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation") in 1815.[[12]](#cite_note-13) The Confederation was dissolved after the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") (1866) in which Prussia defeated the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") and effectively excluded it from taking part in the eventual [unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany").[[13]](#cite_note-14)

Following the war, the states of northern and central Germany united under the leadership of the [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") to form the federal [North German Confederation](/wiki/North_German_Confederation "North German Confederation").[[14]](#cite_note-15) During the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") (1870–1871), the four southern German states of [Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria"), [Württemberg](/wiki/Kingdom_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Kingdom of Württemberg"), [Baden](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden "Grand Duchy of Baden") and [Hesse-Darmstadt](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Hesse "Grand Duchy of Hesse") joined the North German Confederation, which was rechristened the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") with Prussia's victory. The Reichstag and Federal Council ([Bundesrat](/wiki/Bundesrat_(German_Empire) "Bundesrat (German Empire)")) gave the Prussian king the title of [German Emperor](/wiki/German_Emperor "German Emperor") (as of 1 January 1871).[[15]](#cite_note-16) With only relatively minor changes that did not affect its federalized nature, the [North German Constitution](/wiki/North_German_Constitution "North German Constitution") became the [imperial constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_German_Empire "Constitution of the German Empire").[[16]](#cite_note-17) The new [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") included 25 states (three of them [free cities](/wiki/Free_imperial_city "Free imperial city")) plus the imperial territory of [Alsace–Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace%E2%80%93Lorraine "Alsace–Lorraine"), which had been won from France in the war. Within the empire, 65% of the territory and 62% of the population belonged to Prussia.

The [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") at the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") stripped the former German Empire of 12 to 13 percent of its land area and population, the majority of it from Prussia.[[17]](#cite_note-18) Only Alsace–Lorraine, which had never attained full statehood, was wholly lost to Germany. The new [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") remained federal in nature, with a total of 17 states. Seven small states in east-central Germany consolidated into [Thuringia](/wiki/State_of_Thuringia_(1920%E2%80%931952) "State of Thuringia (1920–1952)") in 1920, [Coburg](/wiki/Free_State_of_Coburg "Free State of Coburg") chose to merge into Bavaria (also in 1920), and Prussia absorbed [Pyrmont and Waldeck](/wiki/Free_State_of_Waldeck-Pyrmont "Free State of Waldeck-Pyrmont") (1921 and 1929). During the Weimar period, there were a number of unsuccessful proposals to make radical changes to Germany's state structure, [seven short-lived unrecognized states](/wiki/States_of_the_Weimar_Republic#Unrecognized_states "States of the Weimar Republic"), four of them self-declared soviet republics during the [German revolution of 1918–1919](/wiki/German_revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German revolution of 1918–1919"), plus two separatist republics in the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland") in 1923/24.

After the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") seized power in January 1933, the states were gradually abolished and reduced to provinces under the Nazi regime via the *[Gleichschaltung](/wiki/Gleichschaltung "Gleichschaltung")* process, as the states administratively were largely superseded by the [Nazi *Gau* system](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Nazi_Germany "Administrative divisions of Nazi Germany"). Three changes are of particular note: on 1 January 1934, [Mecklenburg-Schwerin](/wiki/Free_State_of_Mecklenburg-Schwerin "Free State of Mecklenburg-Schwerin") was united with neighbouring [Mecklenburg-Strelitz](/wiki/Free_State_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Free State of Mecklenburg-Strelitz"); and, by the [Greater Hamburg Act](/wiki/Greater_Hamburg_Act "Greater Hamburg Act") (*Groß-Hamburg-Gesetz*) of 1937, the territory of the city-state was extended, while [Lübeck](/wiki/Free_City_of_L%C3%BCbeck "Free City of Lübeck") lost its independence and became part of the Prussian [province of Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Province_of_Schleswig-Holstein "Province of Schleswig-Holstein").

### West Germany, 1945–1990

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: West Germany, 1945–1990")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Deutschland_Bundeslaender_1957.png/250px-Deutschland_Bundeslaender_1957.png)](/wiki/File:Deutschland_Bundeslaender_1957.png)

[West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") (blue) and [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") (red) and [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin") (yellow)

During the [Allied occupation of Germany after World War II](/wiki/Allied_Occupation_Zones_in_Germany "Allied Occupation Zones in Germany"), internal borders were redrawn by the Allied military governments. New states were established in all four zones of occupation: [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)"), [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse"), [Württemberg-Baden](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Baden "Württemberg-Baden"), and [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") in the [American zone](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Germany "Allied-occupied Germany"); [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"), [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein"), [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony"), and [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia") in the [British zone](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Germany "Allied-occupied Germany"); [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate"), [Baden](/wiki/South_Baden "South Baden"), [Württemberg-Hohenzollern](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Hohenzollern "Württemberg-Hohenzollern") and the [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland") – which later received a special status – in the French zone; [Mecklenburg(-Vorpommern)](/wiki/Mecklenburg-Vorpommern "Mecklenburg-Vorpommern"), [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg"), [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony"), [Saxony-Anhalt](/wiki/Saxony-Anhalt "Saxony-Anhalt"), and [Thuringia](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia") in the [Soviet zone](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_zone_of_Germany "Soviet occupation zone of Germany"). No single state comprised more than 30% of either population or territory; this was intended to prevent any one state from being as dominant within Germany as Prussia had been in the past. Initially, only seven of the pre-War states remained: Baden (in part), Bavaria (reduced in size), Bremen, Hamburg, Hesse (enlarged), Saxony, and Thuringia. The states with hyphenated names, such as Rhineland-Palatinate, North Rhine-Westphalia, and Saxony-Anhalt, owed their existence to the occupation powers and were created out of mergers of former Prussian provinces and smaller states.

Former German territory that lay east of the [Oder-Neisse line](/wiki/Oder-Neisse_line "Oder-Neisse line") fell under either Polish or Soviet administration but attempts were made at least symbolically not to abandon sovereignty well into the 1960s. The former [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Prussia "Provinces of Prussia") of [Farther Pomerania](/wiki/Farther_Pomerania "Farther Pomerania"), [East Prussia](/wiki/East_Prussia "East Prussia"), [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia") and [Posen-West Prussia](/wiki/Posen-West_Prussia "Posen-West Prussia") fell under Polish administration with the Soviet Union taking the area around Königsberg (now Kaliningrad), pending a final peace conference with Germany which eventually never took place.[[18]](#cite_note-19) More than 8 million Germans had been [expelled](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_from_Poland_during_and_after_World_War_II "Flight and expulsion of Germans from Poland during and after World War II") from these territories that had formed part of the German-speaking lands for centuries and which mostly did not have sizable Polish minorities before 1945. However, no attempts were made to establish new states in these territories, as they lay outside the jurisdiction of West Germany at that time.

In 1948, the military governors of the three [Western Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") handed over the so-called [Frankfurt Documents](/wiki/Frankfurt_Documents "Frankfurt Documents") to the minister-presidents in the Western occupation zones. Among other things, they recommended revising the boundaries of the West German states in a way that none of them should be too large or too small in comparison with the others.

As the premiers did not come to an agreement on this question, the [Parliamentary Council](/wiki/Parlamentarischer_Rat "Parlamentarischer Rat") was supposed to address this issue. Its provisions are reflected in Article 29 of the [Basic Law](/wiki/Basic_Law_for_the_Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Basic Law for the Federal Republic of Germany"). There was a binding provision for a new delimitation of the federal territory: the Federal Territory must be revised (paragraph 1). Moreover, in territories or parts of territories whose affiliation with a *Land* had changed after 8 May 1945 without a referendum, people were allowed to petition for a revision of the current status within a year after the promulgation of the Basic Law (paragraph 2). If at least one tenth of those entitled to vote in Bundestag elections were in favour of a revision, the federal government had to include the proposal into its legislation. Then a referendum was required in each territory or part of a territory whose affiliation was to be changed (paragraph 3). The proposal should not take effect if within any of the affected territories a majority rejected the change. In this case, the bill had to be introduced again and after passing had to be confirmed by referendum in the Federal Republic as a whole (paragraph 4). The reorganization should be completed within three years after the Basic Law had come into force (paragraph 6). Article 29 states that "the division of the federal territory into Länder may be revised to ensure that each *Land* be of a size and capacity to perform its functions effectively".

In their letter to [Konrad Adenauer](/wiki/Konrad_Adenauer "Konrad Adenauer"), the three western military governors approved the Basic Law but suspended Article 29 until such time as a peace treaty should be concluded. Only the special arrangement for the southwest under Article 118 could enter into force.

Upon its founding in 1949, [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") thus had eleven states. These were reduced to nine in 1952 when three south-western states ([South Baden](/wiki/South_Baden "South Baden"), [Württemberg-Hohenzollern](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Hohenzollern "Württemberg-Hohenzollern"), and [Württemberg-Baden](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Baden "Württemberg-Baden")) merged to form [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg"). From 1957, when the French-occupied [Saar Protectorate](/wiki/Saar_Protectorate "Saar Protectorate") was returned and formed into the [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland"), the Federal Republic consisted of ten states, which are referred to as the "[Old States](/wiki/Old_states_of_Germany "Old states of Germany")" today. West Berlin was under the sovereignty of the Western Allies and neither a Western German state nor part of one. However, it was in many ways integrated with West Germany under a special status.

A new delimitation of the federal territory has been discussed since the Federal Republic was founded in 1949 and even before. Committees and expert commissions advocated a reduction of the number of states; academics ([Werner Rutz](/w/index.php?title=Werner_Rutz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Werner Rutz (page does not exist)"), [Meinhard Miegel](/wiki/Meinhard_Miegel "Meinhard Miegel"), [Adrian Ottnad](/w/index.php?title=Adrian_Ottnad&action=edit&redlink=1 "Adrian Ottnad (page does not exist)"), etc.) and politicians ([Walter Döring](/w/index.php?title=Walter_D%C3%B6ring&action=edit&redlink=1 "Walter Döring (page does not exist)"), [Hans Apel](/wiki/Hans_Apel "Hans Apel"), and others) made proposals – some of them far-reaching – for redrawing boundaries but hardly anything came of these public discussions. Territorial reform is sometimes propagated by the richer states as a means to avoid or reduce [fiscal transfers](/wiki/Equalization_payments "Equalization payments").

#### Establishment of Baden-Württemberg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Establishment of Baden-Württemberg")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Suedweststaat.png/250px-Suedweststaat.png)](/wiki/File:Suedweststaat.png)

The three states that merged to form Baden-Württemberg in 1952

In southwestern Germany, territorial revision seemed to be a top priority since the border between the French and American occupation zones was set along the Autobahn Karlsruhe-Stuttgart-Ulm (today the [A8](/wiki/A8_motorway_(Germany) "A8 motorway (Germany)")). Article 118 stated "The division of the territory comprising [Baden](/wiki/Baden "Baden"), [Württemberg-Baden](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Baden "Württemberg-Baden") and [Württemberg-Hohenzollern](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Hohenzollern "Württemberg-Hohenzollern") into *Länder* may be revised, without regard to the provisions of Article 29, by agreement between the *Länder* concerned. If no agreement is reached, the revision shall be effected by a federal law, which shall provide for an advisory referendum." Since no agreement was reached, a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") was held on 9 December 1951 in four different voting districts, three of which approved the merger ([South Baden](/wiki/South_Baden "South Baden") refused but was overruled, as the result of total votes was decisive). On 25 April 1952, the three former states merged to form Baden-Württemberg.

#### Petitions to reconstitute former states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Petitions to reconstitute former states")]

With the [Paris Agreements](/wiki/London_and_Paris_Conferences "London and Paris Conferences") in 1954, West Germany regained (limited) sovereignty. This triggered the start of the one-year period as set in paragraph 2 of Article 29. As a consequence, eight petitions for referendums were launched, six of which were successful:

* Reconstitution of the [Free State of Oldenburg](/wiki/Free_State_of_Oldenburg "Free State of Oldenburg") 12.9%
* Reconstitution of the [Free State of Schaumburg-Lippe](/wiki/Free_State_of_Schaumburg-Lippe "Free State of Schaumburg-Lippe") 15.3%
* Integration of [Koblenz](/wiki/Koblenz_(region) "Koblenz (region)") and [Trier](/wiki/Trier_(region) "Trier (region)") into [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia") 14.2%
* Reintegration of [Rheinhessen](/wiki/Rheinhessen_(region) "Rheinhessen (region)") into [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") 25.3%
* Reintegration of [Montabaur](/wiki/Montabaur_(region) "Montabaur (region)") into Hesse 20.2%
* Reconstitution of [Baden](/wiki/Republic_of_Baden "Republic of Baden") 15.1%

The last petition was originally rejected by the Federal Minister of the Interior by reference to the referendum of 1951. However, the [Federal Constitutional Court of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Constitutional_Court_of_Germany "Federal Constitutional Court of Germany") ruled that the rejection was unlawful: the population of Baden had the right to a new referendum because the one of 1951 had taken place under different rules from the ones provided for by article 29. In particular, the outcome of the 1951 referendum did not reflect the wishes of the majority of Baden's population.

The two [Palatine](/wiki/Pfalz_(region) "Pfalz (region)") petitions (for a reintegration into Bavaria and integration into Baden-Württemberg) failed with 7.6% and 9.3%. Further requests for petitions (Lübeck, Geesthacht, Lindau, Achberg, and 62 Hessian communities) had already been rejected as inadmissible by the Federal Minister of the Interior or were withdrawn as in the case of Lindau. The rejection was confirmed by the Federal Constitutional Court in the case of Lübeck.

#### Saar: the little reunification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Saar: the little reunification")]

Further information: [Saar Protectorate](/wiki/Saar_Protectorate "Saar Protectorate")

In the [Paris Agreements](/wiki/Paris_Agreements "Paris Agreements") of 23 October 1954, France offered to establish an independent "Saarland", under the auspices of the [Western European Union](/wiki/Western_European_Union "Western European Union") (WEU), but on 23 October 1955 in the [Saar Statute referendum](/wiki/Saar_Statute_referendum,_1955 "Saar Statute referendum, 1955") the Saar electorate rejected this plan by 67.7% to 32.3% (out of a 96.5% turnout: 423,434 against, 201,975 for) despite the public support of Federal German Chancellor [Konrad Adenauer](/wiki/Konrad_Adenauer "Konrad Adenauer") for the plan. The rejection of the plan by the Saarlanders was interpreted as support for the Saar to join the Federal Republic of Germany.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

On 27 October 1956, the [Saar Treaty](/wiki/Saar_Treaty "Saar Treaty") established that Saarland should be allowed to join Germany, as provided by the German constitution. Saarland became part of Germany effective 1 January 1957. The Franco-Saarlander currency union ended on 6 July 1959, when the [Deutsche Mark](/wiki/Deutsche_Mark "Deutsche Mark") was introduced as legal tender in the Saarland.

#### Constitutional amendments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Constitutional amendments")]

Paragraph 6 of Article 29 stated that, if a petition was successful, a referendum should be held within three years. Since the deadline passed on 5 May 1958 without anything happening, the Hesse state government filed a constitutional complaint with the Federal Constitutional Court in October 1958. The complaint was dismissed in July 1961 on the grounds that Article 29 had made the new delimitation of the federal territory an exclusively federal matter. At the same time, the Court reaffirmed the requirement for a territorial revision as a binding order to the relevant constitutional bodies.

The [grand coalition](/wiki/Grand_coalition_(Germany) "Grand coalition (Germany)") decided to settle the 1956 petitions by setting binding deadlines for the required referendums. The referendums in Lower Saxony and Rhineland-Palatinate were to be held by 31 March 1975, and the referendum in Baden was to be held by 30 June 1970. The threshold for a successful vote was set at one-quarter of those entitled to vote in Bundestag elections. Paragraph 4 stated that the vote should be disregarded if it contradicted the objectives of paragraph 1.

In his investiture address, given on 28 October 1969 in Bonn, Chancellor [Willy Brandt](/wiki/Willy_Brandt "Willy Brandt") proposed that the government would consider Article 29 of the Basic Law as a binding order. An expert commission was established, named after its chairman, the former Secretary of State Professor Werner Ernst. After two years of work, the experts delivered their report in 1973. It provided an alternative proposal for the two regions: the north and center-southwest.

In the north, either a single new state consisting of Schleswig-Holstein, Hamburg, Bremen and Lower Saxony should be created (solution A) or two new states, one in the northeast consisting of Schleswig-Holstein, Hamburg and the northern part of Lower Saxony (from [Cuxhaven](/wiki/Cuxhaven "Cuxhaven") to [Lüchow-Dannenberg](/wiki/L%C3%BCchow-Dannenberg "Lüchow-Dannenberg")) and one in the northwest consisting of Bremen and the rest of Lower Saxony (solution B).

In the center and southwest, one alternative was that Rhineland-Palatinate (with the exception of the [Germersheim district](/wiki/Germersheim_(district) "Germersheim (district)") but including the [Rhine-Neckar](/wiki/Rhine-Neckar "Rhine-Neckar") region) should be merged with Hesse and the Saarland (solution C), the district of Germersheim would then become part of Baden-Württemberg. The other alternative was that the Palatinate (including the region of [Worms](/wiki/Worms,_Germany "Worms, Germany")) could be merged with the Saarland and Baden-Württemberg, and the rest of Rhineland-Palatinate would then merge with Hesse (solution D).

Both alternatives could be combined (AC, BC, AD, BD).

At the same time, the commission developed criteria for classifying the terms of Article 29 Paragraph 1. The capacity to perform functions effectively was considered most important, whereas regional, historical, and cultural ties were considered as hardly verifiable. To fulfill administrative duties adequately, a population of at least five million per state was considered as necessary.

After a relatively brief discussion and mostly negative responses from the affected states, the proposals were shelved. Public interest was limited or nonexistent.

The referendum in Baden was held on 7 June 1970. 81.9% of voters decided for Baden to remain part of Baden-Württemberg, only 18.1% opted for the reconstitution of the old state of [Baden](/wiki/Republic_of_Baden "Republic of Baden").

The referendums in [Lower Saxony](/wiki/Lower_Saxony "Lower Saxony") and [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate") were held on 19 January 1975 (the percentages given are the percentages of those eligible who voted in favour):

* reconstitution of the [Free State of Oldenburg](/wiki/Free_State_of_Oldenburg "Free State of Oldenburg") 31%
* reconstitution of the [Free State of Schaumburg-Lippe](/wiki/Free_State_of_Schaumburg-Lippe "Free State of Schaumburg-Lippe") 39.5%
* integration of [Koblenz](/wiki/Koblenz "Koblenz") and [Trier](/wiki/Trier "Trier") into [North Rhine-Westphalia](/wiki/North_Rhine-Westphalia "North Rhine-Westphalia") 13%
* reintegration of Rheinhessen into Hesse 7.1%
* reintegration of Montabaur region into Hesse 14.3%

The votes in Lower Saxony were successful as both proposals were supported by more than 25% of eligible voters. The Bundestag, however, decided that both Oldenburg and Schaumburg-Lippe should remain part of Lower Saxony. The justification was that a reconstitution of the two former states would contradict the objectives of paragraph 1 of article 29 of the constitution. An appeal against the decision was rejected as inadmissible by the Federal Constitutional Court.

On 24 August 1976, the binding provision for a new delimitation of the federal territory was altered into a mere discretionary one. Paragraph 1 of Article 29 was rephrased, with the provision that any state had to be "of a size and capacity to perform its functions effectively" put first.[[20]](#cite_note-21) The option for a referendum in the Federal Republic as a whole (paragraph 4) was abolished, which meant territorial revision was no longer possible against the will of the population affected by it.

### Reunited Germany, 1990–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Reunited Germany, 1990–present")]

[East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") had originally consisted of five states (i.e., Brandenburg, Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, Saxony, Saxony-Anhalt, and Thuringia). In 1952, these states were abolished and the East was divided into [14 administrative districts](/wiki/Administrative_division_of_the_German_Democratic_Republic "Administrative division of the German Democratic Republic") called *Bezirke*. [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")-controlled [East Berlin](/wiki/East_Berlin "East Berlin") – despite officially having the same status as [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin") – was declared East Germany's capital and its 15th district.

The debate on territorial revision restarted shortly before [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification"). While academics (Rutz and others) and politicians (Gobrecht) suggested introducing only two, three, or four states in East Germany, legislation reconstituted the East German states in an arrangement similar to that which they had had before 1952, as the five "[New States](/wiki/New_states_of_Germany "New states of Germany")" on 3 October 1990. The former district of East Berlin joined West Berlin to form the new state of Berlin. Henceforth, the 10 "old states", plus 5 "new states", plus the new state of Berlin, add up to the current 16 states of Germany.

After reunification, the constitution was amended to state that the citizens of the 16 states had successfully achieved the unity of Germany in free self-determination and that the [West German constitution](/wiki/Basic_Law_for_the_Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Basic Law for the Federal Republic of Germany") thus applied to the entire [German people](/wiki/Germans "Germans"). Article 23, which had allowed "any other parts of Germany" to join, was rephrased. It had been used in 1957 to reintegrate the [Saar Protectorate](/wiki/Saar_Protectorate "Saar Protectorate") as the [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland") into the Federal Republic, and this was used as a model for German reunification in 1990. The amended article now defines the participation of the [Federal Council](/wiki/Federal_Council_(Germany) "Federal Council (Germany)") and the 16 German states in matters concerning the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). Article 29 was again modified and provided an option for the states to "revise the division of their existing territory or parts of their territory by agreement without regard to the provisions of paragraphs (2) through (7)". Article 118a was introduced into the Basic Law and provided the possibility for Berlin and Brandenburg to merge "without regard to the provisions of Article 29, by agreement between the two *Länder* with the participation of their inhabitants who are entitled to vote". A state treaty between Berlin and Brandenburg was approved in both parliaments with the necessary two-thirds majority, but in a popular referendum of 5 May 1996, about 63% voted against the merger.

The German states can conclude treaties with foreign countries in matters within their own sphere of competence and with the consent of the federal government (Article 32 of the Basic Law). Typical treaties relate to cultural relationships and economic affairs.

Some states call themselves a "[free state](/wiki/Free_state_(government) "Free state (government)")" (*Freistaat*). It is merely a historic synonym for "republic" and was a description used by most German states after the abolition of monarchy after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). Today, *Freistaat* is associated emotionally with a more independent status, especially in Bavaria. However, it has no legal significance. All sixteen states are represented at the federal level in the *[Bundesrat](/wiki/Bundesrat_(Germany) "Bundesrat (Germany)")* (Federal Council), where their voting power depends on the size of their population.

### Relationship with the Federation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Relationship with the Federation")]

[Federalism is one of the entrenched constitutional principles of Germany](/wiki/Federalism_in_Germany "Federalism in Germany"). Accordingly, the states form a considerable counterweight to the power of the federation. In principle, the power to enact laws lies with the states; the federation can only enact its own laws if the Basic Law explicitly assigns it legislative powers in the respective area. This can be done in two ways:

* The federation can be granted exclusive legislative authority. In this case, the federation alone is authorized to legislate in this area. This applies, for example, to foreign and defense policy.
* The federation can be granted the right to concurrent legislation. In this case, the federation and the states can both enact laws in the respective area; in case of doubt, however, a federal law takes precedence over a state law. This applies, for example, to tax law.

In other areas, only the states are authorized to legislate; this includes, among other things, police law and culture (which in Germany also encompasses the school, vocational training, and university systems).

Even in the areas that the Basic Law assigns to the federal government in whole or in part, the states retain influence on legislation, since the state governments are involved in legislation at the federal level through the Bundesrat. Above all, the federal government can only assume additional powers that have so far resided with the states if the constitution is amended, which requires, among other things, a two-thirds majority in the Bundesrat. The states cannot therefore be disempowered against their will.

### Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/German_state_government_compositions.svg/250px-German_state_government_compositions.svg.png)](/wiki/File:German_state_government_compositions.svg)

Composition of German states' governing coalitions

The federal constitution stipulates that the structure of each Federated State's government must "conform to the principles of republican, democratic, and social government, based on the rule of law" (Article 28). Most of the states are governed by a [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)") led by a *[Ministerpräsident](/wiki/Minister_president_(Germany) "Minister president (Germany)")* (minister-president), together with a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") [legislative body](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") known as the *[Landtag](/wiki/Landtag "Landtag")* (State [Diet](/wiki/Diet_(assembly) "Diet (assembly)")). The states are [parliamentary republics](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic") and the relationship between their legislative and executive branches mirrors that of the federal system: the legislatures are popularly elected for four or five years (depending on the state), and the minister-president is then chosen by a [majority vote](/wiki/Majority_vote "Majority vote") among the *Landtag*'s members. The [minister-president](/wiki/Minister-president "Minister-president") is typically the head of the biggest party of a coalition. The minister-president appoints a cabinet to run the state's agencies and to carry out the executive duties of the state's government. Like in other parliamentary systems, the legislature can dismiss or replace the minister-president after a successful [no-confidence vote](/wiki/No-confidence_vote "No-confidence vote").

The governments in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)") and [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") are referred to as "[senates](/wiki/Senate#Alternative_meanings "Senate")". In the [free states](/wiki/Free_state_(government) "Free state (government)") of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") and [Saxony](/wiki/Free_State_of_Saxony "Free State of Saxony"), the government is referred to as "state government" *(Staatsregierung)*; and in the other states, the government is referred to as "Land government" *(Landesregierung)*. Before 1 January 2000, Bavaria had a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") parliament, with a popularly elected [*Landtag*](/wiki/Landtag_of_Bavaria "Landtag of Bavaria"), and a [Senate](/wiki/Bavarian_Senate "Bavarian Senate") made up of representatives of the state's major social and economic groups. The Senate was abolished following a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") in 1998. The states of Berlin, Bremen, and Hamburg are governed slightly differently from the other states. In each of those cities, the executive branch consists of a Senate of approximately eight, selected by the state's parliament; the senators carry out duties equivalent to those of the ministers in the larger states. The equivalent of the minister-president is the *Senatspräsident* (president of the senate), also commonly referred to as *Bürgermeister* (Mayor) in Bremen, the *Erster Bürgermeister* (first mayor) in Hamburg, and the *Regierender Bürgermeister* (governing mayor) in Berlin. The parliament for Berlin is called the *Abgeordnetenhaus* (House of Representatives), while Bremen and Hamburg both have a *Bürgerschaft*. The parliaments in the remaining 13 states are referred to as *Landtag* (State Parliament).

## Subdivisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Subdivisions")]

[![The vertical (federal) separation of powers across the federal government (white), the states (yellow), and the municipalities (brown).](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Administrative_divisions_of_Germany.svg/330px-Administrative_divisions_of_Germany.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Administrative_divisions_of_Germany.svg "File:Administrative divisions of Germany.svg")

Administrative divisions of Germany (clickable [image](/wiki/File:Administrative_divisions_of_Germany.svg "File:Administrative divisions of Germany.svg"))

The [city-states](/wiki/City-state "City-state") of Berlin and Hamburg are subdivided into [districts](/wiki/District "District"). The City of Bremen consists of two [urban districts](/wiki/List_of_German_urban_districts "List of German urban districts"): [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen "Bremen") and [Bremerhaven](/wiki/Bremerhaven "Bremerhaven"), which are not contiguous. In the other states there are the subdivisions below.

### Area associations (*Landschaftsverbände*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Area associations (Landschaftsverbände)")]

The most populous state of North Rhine-Westphalia is uniquely divided into two area associations (*[Landschaftsverbände](/wiki/Landschaftsverband "Landschaftsverband")*), one for the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland"), and one for [Westphalia](/wiki/Westphalia "Westphalia")-[Lippe](/wiki/Lippe_(district) "Lippe (district)"). This arrangement was meant to ease the friction caused by uniting the two culturally different regions into a single state after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The *Landschaftsverbände* now have very little power.

The constitution of [Mecklenburg-Vorpommern](/wiki/Mecklenburg-Vorpommern "Mecklenburg-Vorpommern") at §75 states the right of [Mecklenburg](/wiki/Mecklenburg "Mecklenburg") and [Vorpommern](/wiki/Vorpommern "Vorpommern") to form *Landschaftsverbände*, although these two constituent parts of the state are not represented in the current administrative division.

### Governmental districts (*Regierungsbezirke*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Governmental districts (Regierungsbezirke)")]

The large states of Baden-Württemberg, Bavaria, Hesse, and North Rhine-Westphalia are divided into governmental districts, or *[Regierungsbezirke](/wiki/Regierungsbezirk "Regierungsbezirk")*.

In Rhineland-Palatinate, these districts were abolished or re-organized on 1 January 2000, in Saxony-Anhalt on 1 January 2004, and in Lower Saxony on 1 January 2005. From 1990 until 2012, Saxony was divided into three districts (called *Direktionsbezirke* since 2008). In 2012, these districts' authorities were merged into one central authority, the *[Landesdirektion Sachsen](/w/index.php?title=Landesdirektion_Sachsen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Landesdirektion Sachsen (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Landesdirektion_Sachsen "de:Landesdirektion Sachsen")]*.

### Administrative districts (*Kreise*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Administrative districts (Kreise)")]

Main article: [Districts of Germany](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany")

See also: [List of districts of Germany](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Germany "List of districts of Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Landkreise%2C_Kreise_und_kreisfreie_St%C3%A4dte_in_Deutschland_2026-01-01.svg/250px-Landkreise%2C_Kreise_und_kreisfreie_St%C3%A4dte_in_Deutschland_2026-01-01.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Landkreise,_Kreise_und_kreisfreie_St%C3%A4dte_in_Deutschland_2026-01-01.svg)

Map of German districts. Yellow districts are urban, while cream districts are suburban or rural.

The [Districts of Germany](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany") *(Kreise)* are administrative districts, and every state except the city-states of [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") and [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") and the state of [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)") consists of "rural districts" *(Landkreise),* District-free Towns/Cities (*Kreisfreie Städte*, in Baden-Württemberg also called "urban districts", or *Stadtkreise*), cities that are districts in their own right, or local associations of a special kind *(Kommunalverbände besonderer Art),* see below. The state [Free Hanseatic City of Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)") consists of two urban districts, while Berlin and Hamburg are states and urban districts at the same time.

As of 2011, there are 295 *Landkreise* and 107 *Kreisfreie Städte*, making 402 districts altogether. Each consists of an elected council and an executive, which is chosen either by the council or by the people, depending on the state, the duties of which are comparable to those of a [county executive](/wiki/County_executive "County executive") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States_of_America "United States of America"), supervising local government administration. The *Landkreise* have primary administrative functions in specific areas, such as highways, hospitals, and public utilities.

Local associations of a special kind are an amalgamation of one or more *Landkreise* with one or more *Kreisfreie Städte* to form a replacement of the aforementioned administrative entities at the district level. They are intended to implement simplification of administration at that level. Typically, a district-free city or town and its urban [hinterland](/wiki/Hinterland "Hinterland") are grouped into such an association, or *Kommunalverband besonderer Art*. Such an organization requires the issuing of special laws by the governing state, since they are not covered by the normal administrative structure of the respective states.

In 2010 only three *Kommunalverbände besonderer Art* exist.

* [District of Hanover](/wiki/Hanover_(district) "Hanover (district)"): formed in 2001 from the rural district of Hanover and the district-free city of [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover").
* *Regionalverband* (district association) of [Saarbrücken](/wiki/Saarbr%C3%BCcken_(district) "Saarbrücken (district)"): formed in 2008 from the *Stadtverband Saarbrücken* (city association of [Saarbrücken](/wiki/Saarbr%C3%BCcken "Saarbrücken")), which was formed in 1974.
* [City region of Aachen](/wiki/Aachen_(district) "Aachen (district)"): formed in 2009 from the rural district of Aachen and the district-free city of [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen").

### Offices (*Ämter*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Offices (Ämter)")]

[Ämter](/wiki/Amt_(country_subdivision) "Amt (country subdivision)") ("offices" or "bureaus"): in some states, there is an administrative unit between the districts and the municipalities, called *Ämter* (singular *Amt*), *Amtsgemeinden*, *Gemeindeverwaltungsverbände*, *Landgemeinden*, *Verbandsgemeinden*, *Verwaltungsgemeinschaften*, or *Kirchspiellandgemeinden*.

### Municipalities (*Gemeinden*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Municipalities (Gemeinden)")]

Main article: [Municipalities of Germany](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Germany "Municipalities of Germany")

[Municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Germany "Municipalities of Germany") (*Gemeinden*): every rural district and every *Amt* is subdivided into [municipalities](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality"), while every urban district is a municipality in its own right. There are (as of 6 March 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=States_of_Germany&action=edit)) 12,141 municipalities, which are the smallest administrative units in Germany. Cities and towns are municipalities as well, also having city rights or town rights (*[Stadtrechte](/wiki/Stadtrecht "Stadtrecht")*). Nowadays, this is mostly just the right to be called a city or town. However, in former times there were many other privileges, including the right to impose local taxes or to allow industry only within city limits. Municipalities have the competence to define the amount of taxes to be paid, esp. facing *Gewerbesteuer* (company tax) and *Grundsteuer* (property tax). Municipalities have the competence to deliver local services of general interest (so called *Kommunale Daseinsvorsorge*).

The number of inhabitants of German municipalities differs greatly, the most populous municipality being [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") with nearly 3.8 million inhabitants, while the least populous municipalities (for instance, [Gröde](/wiki/Gr%C3%B6de "Gröde") in [Nordfriesland](/wiki/Nordfriesland "Nordfriesland")) have less than 10 inhabitants.

The municipalities are ruled by elected councils and by an executive, the mayor, who is chosen either by the council or directly by the people, depending on the state. The "constitution" for the municipalities is created by the states and is uniform throughout a state (except for Bremen, which allows Bremerhaven to have its own constitution).

The municipalities have two major policy responsibilities. First, they administer programs authorized by the federal or state government. Such programs typically relate to youth, schools, public health, and social assistance. Second, Article 28(2) of the Basic Law guarantees the municipalities "the right to regulate on their own responsibility all the affairs of the local community within the limits set by law". Under this broad statement of competence, local governments can justify a wide range of activities. For instance, many municipalities develop and expand the economic infrastructure of their communities through the development of industrial [trading estates](/wiki/Trading_estate "Trading estate").

Local authorities foster cultural activities by supporting local artists, building [arts centres](/wiki/Arts_centres "Arts centres"), and by holding fairs. Local government also provides public utilities, such as gas and electricity, as well as public transportation. The majority of the funding for municipalities is provided by higher levels of government rather than from taxes raised and collected directly by themselves.

In five of the German states, there are [unincorporated areas](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area"), in many cases unpopulated forest and mountain areas, but also four Bavarian lakes that are not part of any municipality. As of 1 January 2005, there were 246 such areas, with a total area of 4167.66 km2 or 1.2% of the total area of Germany. Only four unincorporated areas are populated, with a total population of around 2,000. The table below provides an overview.
